title: Hi, I'm Sabriel!
excerpt: This is my first blog post! I will be sharing my thoughts, experiences, and insights on various topics related to personal growth, tech, and more. Stay tuned for exciting content!
author: Sabriel Adriel San Agustin
content:
Hi there! I'm Sabriel, an IT student, aspiring developer, and cybersecurity enthusiast. I'm driven by curiosity and a love for creating. There's something exciting about taking an idea, turning it into a working project, and pushing myself to solve problems I didn't expect. I've been meaning to start this blog as a space to share what I'm learning, creating, and exploring along the way.

Here's what you can expect to see here:

  • • Experiments and small projects I'm working on, from web tools to design experiments
  • • Stories from learning new technologies, tackling coding challenges, or diving into cybersecurity explorations
  • • Tutorials, walkthroughs, and tips that I hope can help someone else along their journey
  • • Personal reflections on learning tech, growing as a developer, and navigating cybersecurity

I'm still figuring things out, but sharing my journey helps me grow — and maybe inspires someone else along the way.

title: The Reality Behind the Screen: What My First Two Years as an IT Student Taught Me
excerpt: When I first entered college as an IT student, I thought I already understood technology. But during my first and second year, I realized that the reality of IT was much deeper than I expected.
author: Sabriel Adriel San Agustin
content:
When I first entered college as an IT student, I thought I already understood technology. I spent years using computers, exploring the internet, troubleshooting random problems for school, friends and family, and being the "tech person" type of guy — the one interested in computers, troubleshooting problems, exploring software, and learning random things about technology online.


Because of that, I entered college feeling confident. I thought IT would mostly be about coding cool applications and learning advanced technologies immediately.


But during my first and second year, I realized that the reality of IT was much deeper than I expected.


First Year, First Semester — Starting From the Basics


During my first semester, we were mostly introduced to the logic and foundations behind technology. We learned the history of computing, basic concepts of computers, and simple programming using C++ in the Turbo C IDE.


At first, I honestly felt underwhelmed.


I expected advanced programming immediately, but instead, we focused on logic, problem-solving, and introductory concepts. We also had several minor subjects that felt unrelated to technology, which made me question what IT college life would really be like.


Still, looking back now, I realize those fundamentals were important.


Programming was not just about typing code. It was about learning how to think logically.


The Holiday Break That Changed My Direction


During our holiday break, I became obsessed with self-learning.


I spent a lot of time watching tutorials, taking free online courses, and collecting certificates. At that time, I believed certificates would help me get employed quickly in the future.


I explored different fields in IT:

  • • UI/UX Design
  • • Cybersecurity
  • • Web Development

I wanted to learn everything.


But eventually, I became overwhelmed.


The more I explored technology, the more I realized how massive the IT industry really was. Every field had its own tools, roadmaps, communities, and required skills.


That was the first time I understood that specialization matters.


I realized that if I wanted to become truly good at something, I could not master everything all at once.


Even then, part of me still wanted to try learning it all.


Second Semester — Real Programming Experience


When the second semester started, I felt more prepared compared to before.


Because of my self-study during the break, I already had basic knowledge about several areas of technology. This time, our classes became more hands-on and practical.


We started using Visual Studio and learned Visual Basic .NET for application development.


This was the phase where I became genuinely interested in programming.


Creating applications, designing interfaces, and seeing programs actually work gave me motivation to continue learning. For the first time, programming felt real instead of purely theoretical.


At the same time, I continued learning outside school.


I watched YouTube tutorials almost daily, followed TikTok tips and tech creators, read guides online, and watched vlogs from successful people working in the tech industry.


I became addicted to learning.


Exploring Cybersecurity


Cybersecurity was not actually new to me.


Even back in high school, I was already aware of hacking, penetration testing, and ethical hacking communities online. I found the field interesting because it looked challenging, technical, and different from typical programming.


But back then, I never really had the courage to seriously learn it.


The field felt intimidating.


Whenever I saw people discussing Linux, networking, exploits, scripting, and advanced technical concepts, I felt like cybersecurity was only for extremely skilled people. Because of that, I mostly stayed as an observer instead of actually trying to study it deeply.


It was only during my first year in college that I finally attempted to explore cybersecurity more seriously.


As I continued self-studying outside school, I became more curious about red teaming, penetration testing, and bug bounty hunting. The idea of understanding systems deeply, finding vulnerabilities, and thinking like an attacker made the field even more interesting to me.


I spent time watching cybersecurity content, reading beginner guides, exploring tools, and learning how ethical hackers approach security testing. I also became interested in bug bounty hunting because it felt like a practical way to apply cybersecurity skills in real-world systems.


The more I researched cybersecurity, the more I realized how broad the field truly is.


I started seeing roadmaps involving:

  • • Networking
  • • Linux
  • • Operating Systems
  • • Programming
  • • Scripting
  • • Cloud Computing
  • • System Administration
  • • Web Security

I also saw many professionals explaining that cybersecurity — especially penetration testing — is usually not an entry-level career.


That realization overwhelmed me.


I understood that before specializing in cybersecurity, I first needed stronger foundations and deeper knowledge in different areas of technology.


For the first time, I realized that passion alone was not enough. The field required patience, consistency, and years of continuous learning.


Instead of discouraging me, that realization changed my perspective.


I understood that if I truly wanted to pursue cybersecurity in the future, I first needed to build my skills step by step and strengthen my understanding of technology as a whole.


Switching Toward UI/UX and Web Development


Eventually, I shifted my attention toward UI/UX design and web development.


Compared to cybersecurity, these fields felt more natural to me.


I already had a background in graphic design during my high school days because I served as the designer, scribe, and layout artist of our organization in the Boy Scouts of the Philippines.


Because of that experience, UI/UX design felt familiar and enjoyable.


I started learning:

  • • Figma
  • • Visual Studio Code
  • • HTML
  • • CSS

I learned them all simultaneously.


This was also the phase where I truly felt myself leveling up technically. Compared to my first semester, I finally felt like I was building real and practical skills.


Designing interfaces and creating websites felt exciting.


Of course, there were still stressful moments.


There were times when:

  • • Syntax errors frustrated me for hours
  • • I forgot simple CSS properties
  • • My layouts looked bad despite multiple revisions
  • • My perfectionism slowed me down

But despite those frustrations, I genuinely enjoyed the process.


Throughout the school year break, I spent most of my time learning, practicing, watching tutorials, and improving my skills in design and frontend development.


For the first time, I felt truly connected to the field I was studying.


Reflection


Looking back at my first year as an IT student, I realized that technology is much deeper than I originally thought.


I entered college believing that being interested in computers meant I already knew a lot.


But my first year taught me that IT is not only about technology — it is also about patience, consistency, self-learning, and adapting to challenges.


There were moments where I felt confident, overwhelmed, motivated, and lost all at the same time.


But I think that is part of the journey of being an IT student.


Right now, I still do not fully know where I will end up in the tech industry.


But after my first year, one thing became clear to me:


Learning technology is not a race to know everything immediately. It is a continuous process of exploring, struggling, improving, and slowly finding where you truly belong.


Second Year, First Semester — Learning Through Building


Entering my second year felt different compared to my first year.


I was no longer completely new to IT. I already explored different technologies, learned multiple tools outside school, and spent countless hours self-learning during breaks.


Because of that, second year felt more casual.


I simply went to school, attended classes, completed requirements, and continued learning.


What excited me the most during this semester were system projects.


Whenever instructors announced that we would build something, I genuinely became excited because I knew I could benefit from the process of creating. Building projects always felt like the fastest way for me to learn.


Of course, there were still minor subjects that felt boring and unrelated sometimes.


But that was already something I accepted.


During this semester, we also learned more about Microsoft Office applications in detail. While many students considered these basic skills, I understood their importance because these tools are still heavily used professionally.


Another subject that excited me was networking.


For the first time, I felt like I was learning something that could directly help me achieve my long-term goal in cybersecurity.


We started learning networking concepts, used Packet Tracer, and created network designs for our school.


At the same time, my learning outside school never stopped.


Even during this semester, I continued exploring cybersecurity fundamentals on my own.


I started using TryHackMe and completed multiple beginner labs and learning paths. For the first time, instead of simply watching cybersecurity content, I was actually interacting with systems, reading documentation, solving exercises, and understanding how different technologies worked together.


Although I was still a beginner, these small experiences made cybersecurity feel less intimidating.


This was also the period where I discovered OSINT.


Learning that information gathering itself could become an entire skillset fascinated me.


I found it interesting how publicly available information, search techniques, and investigative thinking could be used to understand systems, organizations, and people more deeply.


Unlike before, where cybersecurity felt impossible to approach, OSINT made me feel like I could actually start somewhere.


Until now, OSINT remains one of the things that continues motivating me to explore cybersecurity further.


This was one of the moments where I realized something:


A large portion of what I knew about technology did not only come from school.


Most of it came from self-learning.


School introduced concepts.


Self-learning expanded them.


Another Holiday Break — JavaScript Era


When the holiday break arrived again, I immediately returned to self-learning.


This time, I focused heavily on JavaScript.


I already had some theoretical knowledge because I previously watched videos discussing the language, but I never truly had enough hands-on experience building with it.


So I committed myself to learning properly.


I followed a 22-hour JavaScript course on YouTube.


What I thought would take a few days ended up taking multiple weeks.


The difficult part was not watching the lessons.


The difficult part was the exercises.


There were many moments where I felt stupid because I could not solve simple exercises by myself.


At first, I promised myself that I would not use AI while learning because I wanted to solve everything independently.


Eventually, my patience gave up.


I started using AI.


Not to completely solve problems for me, but because sometimes I simply could not understand why my solution was wrong.


At the same time, I also continued improving my frontend skills.


Alongside JavaScript, I started learning Tailwind CSS.


Compared to writing traditional CSS from scratch, Tailwind felt different.


At first, seeing long class names everywhere looked confusing, but as I continued building small projects, I started understanding why many developers preferred it.


Learning JavaScript and Tailwind CSS simultaneously made me realize something:


Building websites is much more than simply making things look good.


You also need to understand interactions, logic, structure, responsiveness, and how everything works together.


Even now, I still do not consider myself confident or advanced in JavaScript.


I still search syntax.


I still forget things.


I still encounter problems that confuse me.


But compared to before, I now understand the logic behind the language and can build multiple projects using it.


And honestly, that felt like progress.


Second Semester — Burnout and Confusion


During the second semester, we had subjects involving e-commerce and visual graphic design.


This was interesting for me because we created multiple graphic materials including posters, layouts, clothing designs, and other creative outputs.


We also prototyped our e-commerce store using Figma.


Because I already enjoyed design, these activities felt enjoyable rather than stressful.


We also learned the basics of databases using Microsoft Access.


At the same time, networking continued.


This time, we explored deeper concepts and became more hands-on with network connections and internet-related technologies.


Despite learning many interesting things, this semester was different.


I was tired.


I was burned out.


For a while, I felt lost.


I could not figure out where I truly wanted to be.


Part of me still wanted cybersecurity.


That never changed.


But another part of me understood that rushing directly into cybersecurity without stronger foundations would only make things harder.


That realization confused me.


I knew where I wanted to end up.


I just did not know exactly how to get there yet.


So instead of forcing myself to immediately specialize, I decided to continue learning other areas first.


Not because I gave up on cybersecurity.


But because I believed these experiences would eventually help me get there.


Reflection


Second year taught me something different from first year.


First year taught me that technology is much bigger than I expected.


Second year taught me that growth is not always clear.


Sometimes progress looks like building projects.


Sometimes progress looks like being confused.


Sometimes progress looks like changing directions while still keeping the same destination.


Even now, I still do not fully know where my journey in technology will lead.


But halfway through college, one thing became clear:


Learning technology is not simply about learning tools. It is about continuously learning even when you feel lost.
